questions.q: On the open-set lattice $\tau = \{\varnothing, \{x\}, \{y\}, \{x,y\}, X\}$ of $X = \{x,y,z\}$, what is $\{x\} \Rightarrow \{y\}$?
questions.explain: $a \Rightarrow b$ is the largest $c$ with $a \wedge c \le b$. Test each candidate: $\{x\} \cap \{y\} = \varnothing \subseteq \{y\}$ ✓, $\{x\} \cap \{x,y\} = \{x\} \not\subseteq \{y\}$, $\{x\}\cap X = \{x\}\not\subseteq\{y\}$. So the largest $c$ that works is $\{y\}$ itself.
questions.hint: Compute $\{x\} \cap c$ for each $c$ in the lattice and check which lie inside $\{y\}$ — pick the largest.

questions.q: Which of the following finite lattices are Heyting algebras?
questions.explain: A finite lattice is Heyting iff it is distributive (then $b\Rightarrow c$ exists as the join of all $x$ with $x\wedge b\le c$). $N_5$ and $M_3$ are the two forbidden non-distributive sublattices of Birkhoff's theorem, so they are not Heyting. Boolean algebras are distributive (in fact complemented). Finite chains are distributive.
questions.hint: Heyting ⇔ distributive (for finite lattices). The two non-distributive 5-element lattices are exactly $N_5$ and $M_3$.

questions.q: In a Heyting algebra, which identity holds for every element $a$?
questions.explain:
$
eg
eg
eg a = 
eg a$ is provable in intuitionist logic: from $a \le 
eg
eg a$ apply $
eg$ (which reverses) to get $
eg
eg
eg a \le 
eg a$; combine with the dual instance. The first two are LEM/DNE — equivalent to being Boolean. The fourth fails when $a 
e  ot$.
questions.hint: Triple negation collapses to single negation in any Heyting algebra — but double negation does not collapse to identity.

questions.q: What is the subobject classifier $\Omega$ in the topos $\mathbf{Set}$?
questions.explain: In $\mathbf{Set}$, subobjects of $X$ are subsets, and a subset is determined by its indicator function $X \to \{\bot,\top\}$. So $\Omega = \{\bot,\top\}$ with $\mathrm{true}(*)=\top$. Two truth values — exactly the data needed for classical Boolean logic.
questions.hint: Subobjects of a set are subsets; an indicator function takes how many values?

questions.q: In the topos $G$-$\mathbf{Set}$ of left $G$-sets, $\Omega$ as a $G$-set has underlying set:
choices: The set of normal subgroups of $G$
The set of conjugacy classes in $G$
questions.explain: A subobject of the terminal $G$-set $\{*\}$ is a $G$-stable subset of $\{*\}$ — but the elements of $\Omega$ in $G$-$\mathbf{Set}$ are sieves on the unique object of the one-object groupoid $G$, which correspond to subgroups of $G$. The action of $g$ on $H \le G$ is conjugation $H \mapsto gHg^{-1}$.
questions.hint: Think of $G$ as a one-object groupoid; $\Omega(c)$ in a presheaf topos is the sieves on $c$.

questions.q: Match each Heyting operation on $\Omega$ with the subobject of $\Omega \times \Omega$ it classifies.
right: the order relation $\le$ on $\Omega$, the equaliser of $\wedge$ and $\pi_1$
questions.explain: $\wedge$ classifies the singleton $(\top,\top)$; $\vee$ classifies the union of the two true-lines; $\Rightarrow$ classifies the order $\le$. See Mac Lane–Moerdijk IV.8.
questions.hint: Conjunction is true only when both coordinates are true; implication is true when the first coordinate is below the second.

hard.q: In $\mathrm{Sh}(\mathbb{R})$, the section $\Omega(U)$ over an open $U \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ is:
choices: the set of open subsets of $U$
the set of all subsets of $U$
hard.explain: In $\mathrm{Sh}(X)$, $\Omega$ is the sheaf $U \mapsto \{\text{open subsets of } U\}$ with $\mathrm{true}_U = U$. A subobject of a sheaf $F$ over $U$ is a sub-sheaf, picked out by 'on which open does each section live.' This makes $\Omega(U)$ a Heyting algebra under $\cap, \cup,$ and the formula $V \Rightarrow W = \mathrm{int}(U \setminus V \cup W)$.
hard.hint: Sub-sheaves of the terminal sheaf over $U$ are determined by the maximal open on which the section is defined.

hard.explain: $\neg p = p \Rightarrow \bot$. The map pairs $p$ with the constant $\bot$ (via the unique $\Omega \to 1$ then $\bot\colon 1 \to \Omega$) and applies $\Rightarrow$. There is no canonical 'swap automorphism' on $\Omega$ in non-Boolean toposes.

questions.q: Match each piece of the internal language to its categorical interpretation.
right: right adjoint $\Pi_{\pi}$ to pullback along $\pi\colon\Gamma\times A \to \Gamma$
questions.explain: Closed terms are global sections; formulas are characteristic maps (or equivalently subobjects); $\exists$ is the image / left adjoint; $\forall$ is the right adjoint to pullback. This is the standard Lawvere adjoint correspondence for quantifiers.

questions.q: The formula $\forall x.\,(P(x) \to Q(x))$ is interpreted in $\mathrm{Sub}(1)$ as:
questions.explain: First compute $\{P \to Q\} = \{P\} \Rightarrow \{Q\}$ in $\mathrm{Sub}(A)$. Then quantify universally: $\forall x.\,$ becomes $\Pi_\pi$, the right adjoint to $\pi^*\colon \mathrm{Sub}(1) \to \mathrm{Sub}(A)$. So the answer is $\Pi_\pi(\{P\} \Rightarrow \{Q\})$.
questions.hint: Implication first, then $\forall$ as right adjoint to pullback.

questions.q: Provability $\Gamma \vdash \varphi$ in the Mitchell–Bénabou logic translates to which categorical condition?
questions.explain: $\Gamma \vdash \varphi$ means: in the context $\Gamma$, $\varphi$ is always true. In subobject terms, the subobject $\{\Gamma \mid \varphi\}$ is everything — equal to the top of $\mathrm{Sub}(\llbracket\Gamma\rrbracket)$. The classifying map $\llbracket\varphi\rrbracket$ is then $\mathrm{true}\circ !\colon \llbracket\Gamma\rrbracket \to 1 \to \Omega$.
questions.hint: A formula provable from $\Gamma$ holds on the entire context object.

questions.q: On the 2-world Kripke model with $C = (a \to b)$ and valuation $\llbracket p\rrbracket(a)=\varnothing$, $\llbracket p\rrbracket(b)=\{*\}$, which of the following are forced at world $b$?
questions.explain: At $b$: (0) $b \Vdash p$ since $\llbracket p rbracket(b) e \varnothing$. (1) $b ot\Vdash eg p$ since $\mathrm{id}_b$ forces $p$. (2) $b \Vdash p \vee eg p$ via the left disjunct. (3) $b \Vdash eg eg p o p$: every accessible $Y o b$ that forces $ eg eg p$ also forces $p$ — direct. (4) $b \Vdash p o eg eg p$: an intuitionist tautology. So everything except $ eg p$ is forced at $b$. Note that LEM holds at the maximal world $b$ but fails at the lower world $a$.
questions.hint: At the highest world, you can verify $p$ directly; only $\neg p$ requires no future to ever satisfy $p$, which fails.

questions.q: The Kripke–Joyal clause for implication $\varphi \to \psi$ at $X$ is:
choices: there exists $f\colon Y \to X$ with $Y \Vdash \varphi$ and $Y \Vdash \psi$
questions.explain: Kripke–Joyal $\to$ quantifies over every generalised element $Y \to X$ — this is the persistence/stability condition. Without this universal closure, $\to$ would not be invariant under restriction along arrows, and the clauses wouldn't be sound for the topos's internal logic.
questions.hint: Implication in intuitionist Kripke semantics persists along the accessibility relation — the clause must quantify over all accessible worlds.

questions.q: In a sheaf topos $\mathrm{Sh}(X)$, the Kripke–Joyal clause for $\exists y.\,\varphi$ at an open $U$ becomes:
choices: there is a section $b$ on $U$ with $U \Vdash \varphi(b)$
there is a covering family $\{U_i \to U\}$ and sections $b_i$ on $U_i$ with $U_i \Vdash \varphi(b_i)$
for every refinement $\{U_i \to U\}$ and every section $b$, $U_i \Vdash \varphi(b)$
$\varphi$ is true on the stalk at every point of $U$
questions.explain: Existential quantification in sheaf forcing is 'locally a witness exists': there is a covering family on which the witness can be chosen. This is why constructive analysis builds objects locally and glues them by descent rather than picking global sections.
questions.hint: Sheaves are about local data; existential is local existence of a witness.

hard.q: Consider the 3-world model $C$ with objects $a, b, c$ and arrows $a \to b, a \to c$ (no arrow $b \leftrightarrow c$). Let $\llbracket p\rrbracket(b) = \{*\}$, $\llbracket p\rrbracket(a) = \llbracket p\rrbracket(c) = \varnothing$. Where does $\neg\neg p$ hold?
hard.explain:
$
eg
eg p$ at $b$: every $Y 	o b$ has $Y 
ot\Vdash 
eg p$? The only arrow into $b$ is $\mathrm{id}_b$; $b \Vdash p$ so $b 
ot\Vdash 
eg p$. So $b \Vdash 
eg
eg p$. ✓ At $a$: consider $a 	o c$. We have $c \Vdash 
eg p$ (the only arrow into $c$ is $\mathrm{id}_c$, and $c 
ot\Vdash p$). So there's an accessible world where $
eg p$ holds, hence $a 
ot\Vdash 
eg
eg p$. At $c$: $c \Vdash 
eg p$ already, so $c 
ot\Vdash 
eg
eg p$. Only at $b$.
hard.hint:
$
eg
eg p$ at $X$ means: every accessible $Y$ has some $Z 	o Y$ where $Z \Vdash p$. The branching at $a$ towards $c$ kills $
eg
eg p$ at $a$.

hard.q: The Kripke–Joyal clause for disjunction in a topos requires a cover $\{f_i\colon X_i \to X\}$. In a presheaf topos $\widehat{C}$, what is a cover of a representable $X = y(c)$?
choices: the identity $\mathrm{id}\colon y(c) \to y(c)$ alone — there are no proper covers
any family $\{y(d_i) \to y(c)\}$ that is jointly epimorphic
any family of arrows $d_i \to c$ in $C$
hard.explain: In a presheaf topos $\widehat{C}$ (no Grothendieck topology), the only cover of a representable $y(c)$ is the trivial cover $\{\mathrm{id}\}$. This is why Kripke–Joyal forcing on $\widehat{C}$ collapses to the classical Kripke clauses (no genuine 'covering' refinement). Switching to sheaves $\mathrm{Sh}(C, J)$ on a non-trivial site adds genuine covers and is what enables descent.
hard.hint: Presheaf toposes have no Grothendieck topology beyond the trivial one — covers are trivial.

lem-failure.title: Why LEM fails: double negation in toposes

questions.q: In which presheaf topos does LEM fail?
choices: $\widehat{*}$ (the topos of presheaves on the one-object terminal category)
$\widehat{(a \to b)}$ (presheaves on the two-object category with one non-identity arrow)
$\widehat{G}$ for $G$ a group, viewed as a one-object groupoid
questions.explain: $\widehat{*}$ is just $\mathbf{Set}$ (Boolean). $\widehat{G}$ is $G$-$\mathbf{Set}$, also Boolean (since $G$ is a groupoid). $\widehat{(a\to b)}$ is non-Boolean: $\Omega(a)$ has 3 elements (the sieves $\varnothing, \{a\}, \{a, a\to b\}$ — really the latter two), and the 3-element chain is the smallest non-Boolean Heyting algebra. LEM fails. Theorem: $\widehat{C}$ is Boolean iff $C$ is a groupoid.
questions.hint: $\widehat{C}$ is Boolean iff $C$ is a groupoid — the arrow $a \to b$ is not invertible.

questions.q: Select all toposes in which the law of excluded middle holds (i.e. all Boolean toposes).
questions.explain: $\mathbf{Set}$ and $G$-$\mathbf{Set}$ are Boolean (the Boolean groupoid case). $\mathrm{Sh}_{\neg\neg}$ is by construction the largest Boolean subtopos. $\mathrm{Sh}(X)$ is Boolean iff $X$ is discrete (every subset open ⇒ open-set lattice is the powerset, which is Boolean). $\mathrm{Sh}(\mathbb{R})$ is non-Boolean (the open-set lattice has non-trivial boundary). $\widehat{(a\to b)}$ is non-Boolean.
questions.hint: Boolean toposes: $\mathbf{Set}$, $G$-$\mathbf{Set}$, $\mathrm{Sh}_{\neg\neg}$, sheaves on discrete spaces — and not much else.

questions.q:
The double-negation topology $
eg
eg\colon \Omega 	o \Omega$ on a topos $\mathcal{E}$ produces a subtopos $\mathrm{Sh}_{
eg
eg}(\mathcal{E}) \hookrightarrow \mathcal{E}$. What is its defining property?
choices: It is the smallest subtopos containing all $\neg\neg$-closed subobjects
It is the largest Boolean subtopos of $\mathcal{E}$
It is the topos of $\neg$-fixpoints of $\Omega$
questions.hint: Double-negation 'rounds down' all subobjects to their $\neg\neg$-stable parts, exactly the ones that obey LEM.

hard.q: Which subobjects of $\mathbb{R}$ in $\mathrm{Sh}(\mathbb{R})$ are $\neg\neg$-stable (i.e. equal to their double-negation)?
choices: The regular open subsets — those equal to the interior of their closure
hard.explain:
$
eg
eg U = \mathrm{int}(\overline{U})$ in the open-set lattice. So $U = 
eg
eg U$ iff $U = \mathrm{int}(\overline{U})$, the definition of a regular open set. The regular opens form a complete Boolean algebra, the Booleanisation of the Heyting algebra of opens.

hard.q: The classical theorem 'every set has a cardinality' (every set is in bijection with an initial ordinal) requires the axiom of choice. In a topos $\mathcal{E}$, what is the relationship between AC and LEM?
choices: They are equivalent in any topos
hard.explain: Diaconescu's theorem (1975): if every epi splits internally in a topos $\mathcal{E}$, then $\mathcal{E}$ is Boolean. Constructive proof: from a 2-element quotient $\{0,1\}/{\sim}$ where $\sim$ depends on a proposition $\varphi$, internal AC gives a section that decides $\varphi$. The converse (LEM ⇒ AC) is false: $G$-$\mathbf{Set}$ is Boolean but AC fails.
hard.hint: There's a famous theorem of Diaconescu connecting choice and excluded middle in any topos.

questions.q: Which of the following logical connectives are always preserved by the inverse image $f^*$ of a geometric morphism $f\colon \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{E}$?
questions.explain: Geometric logic is the fragment $\{\top, \bot, \wedge, \bigvee, \exists, =\}$. $f^*$ preserves finite limits ($\wedge$, $=$) and arbitrary colimits ($\bigvee$, $\exists$ as image). $\Rightarrow$, $\forall$, $\neg$ are right-adjoint constructions; left adjoints don't commute with right adjoints in general.
questions.hint: Geometric formulas use $\wedge, \bigvee, \exists, =$ — and only those — because $f^*$ preserves finite limits and arbitrary colimits.

questions.q: Why does $f^*$ preserve $\bigvee$ but not $\bigwedge$ (infinitary conjunction)?
choices: $f^*$ has a right adjoint, so it preserves all colimits but not all limits
$\bigwedge$ is not a meaningful operation in any topos
$f^*$ does preserve both — the question is malformed
questions.explain: $f^* \dashv f_*$, so $f^*$ is a left adjoint and preserves all colimits — including infinitary unions $\bigvee$. It only preserves finite limits, not arbitrary ones; $\bigwedge$ over an infinite index is an infinite limit, not preserved.
questions.hint: Left adjoints commute with colimits; $f^*$ is the left adjoint of the geometric morphism.

questions.q: If $\mathcal{T}$ is a geometric theory and $\varphi \vdash_x \psi$ is a geometric sequent provable in the classifying topos $\mathrm{Set}[\mathcal{T}]$, then for any topos $\mathcal{F}$ with a $\mathcal{T}$-model $M$:
choices: $\varphi \vdash_x \psi$ may fail in $\mathcal{F}$ unless $\mathcal{F}$ is Boolean
the sequent must be re-proved in $\mathcal{F}$ from scratch
questions.explain: This is geometric soundness: a $\mathcal{T}$-model in $\mathcal{F}$ corresponds to a geometric morphism $\mathcal{F} \to \mathrm{Set}[\mathcal{T}]$; $f^*$ preserves geometric formulas, so anything provable geometrically in the classifying topos transports along $f^*$ to $\mathcal{F}$. This is the central technical reason for restricting to geometric logic.
questions.hint: The whole point of the geometric fragment: it transports along arbitrary geometric morphisms, including those classifying models.
